speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six. You were telling me how you got the new Jewish friends down to the basement now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they are there, and finally their painting is finished now. And I put them into the servant quarters. And I was thinking that's a beautiful place, because there was a little bathtub-- you could heated water, and there was a little stove, and there was a little tiny like a closet. They prop up the closet--water closet, but then they did have like storage for marmalade, for all the things, groceries and things. One of these room, we made it called, honeymoon hotel for the married couple. We figure out-- that would be perfect, beautiful. And-- and tried to think we will survive. Well, I asked the Major if I may move to the servant's quarters. He said, oh, no. You move by the kitchen. In the cellar, I am bringing my orderly, a surgeon.

speaker: interviewee
text: When i heard that, my feet were just shaking. I said, my gosh, now what will I do? The people are here. So quick, I said, Major, I was raped by Russian soldiers when I was not even kissed by a boy. I am scared of men.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a man in his late 60s. I was pretty girl. So when I told him, he said, oh, that's the reason I never did see you flirting or anything. I said, I am scared of men. Please. I do everything-- I clean your shoes, I wash your shirts. Don't bring them in. I am scared. Give me the opportunity.

words.text: I am scared of men. Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally he said, well, I want to have big parties, and you know-- and I will-- my mother did teach me everything. She was a great entertainer. Please give me the opportunity. Pleaded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally he said, OK, we will try. But if you cannot do it, I will get my-- I get the orderly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I was telling to my friends in downstairs, two of them, I think it was a-- yes-- it was Francis. And then another man said, Irene, this building was built by a Jewish architect, a Mr. Shapkov. He said-- I am pretty sure that there must be another hiding place. Because we noticed, as the Germans would go from Germany to Poland to Ukraine, there was something done in the garden in this. So we start searching. Major still did not really move in. And we find it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was another hiding place. Right underneath coal chute was a wooden wall. When you moved couple of the boards, exposed a passage. A very shallow passage, and to the underneath the gazebo. And underneath the gazebo was a hiding place. There was 9 by 9 octagon building, with a tall stone foundation, in which there were many little air vents cleverly hidden. There were bushes all around. Dr. Lipszits was smallest in the size. He did go first to check it out. And he come back-- he said, we survive, we have a place. We'll survive. We did have to evacuate the rodents. There were rats, or mice, and rats, we evacuated. There were not floor, but we put it-- some of the cover. You know, not carpet, but we put the blanket, and we put the pail with fresh water, a pail for waste. We put some biscuit, we put this. And we're trying to see how long it will take to go from one place to another. We could not walk on the-- we could not even, on the knees. There were like stepladder on the floor, and you had to pull yourself only. But we knew we will survive. I knew we will survive now. We were so happy with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Major did have the big parties. Open house, the [NON-ENGLISH] come, and the German secretaries. And they hated me like poison, the German secretaries, because they were jealous that the head of the you know-- so they're giving me all kinds of very hard time. But who you think prepared the parties? I was so scared that-- that he would send the cooks, or the-- some for this to run for somebody to prepare. So I ask him if he would trust me and give me the opportunity. I said, my mother is the greatest-- was greater-- I know. So he said, OK, if you cannot do it, you know. OK. So far, you did wonderful. So I went with Schultz, we brought all the food for the parties. And my Jewish friends prepare hors d'oeuvres, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: The tables were set beautifully. You know, drinks-- I-- Henry Weinbaum was great. So they say, put wine for this and that. And they say, they will prepare for themselves. They know what they want to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: So with everything prepared. And then, Major thought that I am magician. And I told him, I told you my mother did, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember a few times, it was such a big trouble. When the opening was, the open house. The SS men come with another fraulein. And they were drinking, they were playing piano, they were doing, there was noise. And my people, they were under the gazebo. Ida did have a cold. She was coughing, so she took a pillow with her, when she went there. And I am by the kitchen sink. Of a second, I did see the Gestapo man with his fraulein, they walking to the gazebo. And other were-- there were four rooms upstairs. And then they just, after that, everybody with his fraulein, they disappeared. Well, I was so scared that they will not know that there would be many people. So I put a tray, put the two glasses of wine and hors d'oeuvres, and ran out and yell from far. [NON-ENGLISH], I have something for you and your lady. Well, he was so mad. He was not dressed properly. He says, Fraulein Gut, I did not ask you for anything. But I knew that they know. And so they took the-- he got up and went to the room, too. I am in the kitchen again. [NON-ENGLISH] has fed one of the officers-- a fat guy, mean-- he come from the back, and he try to carry me in a room, too. Well, I didn't want to be raped. But I was-- Major said, I had to-- if I cannot do it everything, he will bring in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that time while he was carrying me, I knock with my foot a vase in the entry hall. It was a little table with a big vase. And the vase fell down, and it made it such a crack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we start seeing all the doors opening. And the Major come with the two German secretaries, with the lipstick all over his face. And I say, Herr Major, please forgive me. I broke the vase. I was clumsy. I could not even tell him, you know, about-- because Herr Hess he was the chief, you know. And I could not tell him, you know. And so that was all, you know. I cleaned that up. Now the time is coming that Ida Heller, they were married before the war. They told them not to have the children. She have a want to talk to me. She said, I am pregnant, expecting baby. Everybody is scared. Dr. Lipszits gave me prescription. He said you have to fill it. We have to abort the baby. We could do it. Klara was a nurse. Dr. Lipszits was a medic. But I could not do it, because I did see the little children going to death alone screaming, Mama, Mama. I could not do it. It was like in kaleidoscope, I could see the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I pleaded. I--for some reason, I have such a strong belief, nobody has right to kill or murder. I have a belief that we survive-- the God put me in the place there. It's beyond shadow of the doubt, I knew, I did not do alone. That was my destiny.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I say, Hitler will not have your baby. You will not have abortion. You will have the baby when you'll be free. Finally, I pleaded for the life of the baby, and they listened to me. And we were surviving. Doing what naturally in going to the forest and doing things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I overheard-- Before you go on, Irene, tell me again the Major's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edward Riggerman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you know about him? What did you know then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know about him that he was at-- he have two sons that were Gestapo. But they were in Germany. He was only alone. And I think he was divorced or separated from the wife. That's what I knew. He did have an ulcer. So at the beginning, I cooked for him special food and this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but I did not see him to kill anyone, murder anyone, or to even hurt anyone. He was the old efficient German, you know? They put uniform-- he was not professional solider. They put uniform on him because he was an expert with the ammunition and this. How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was around 67 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any kindness from him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did see that he always was fighting with the Gestapo men. He say, why are you doing that? I need my people. And when they were working double shifts, he told Schultz to give-- give extra food and this. He was not a bad man. I mean, he was naturally this, but I will not-- I did not see him kill or murder anyone. But he was friends with the Gestapo men. And the Gestapo men were-- was coming always. That is the thing. And another thing, I felt a woman, no matter how young, I knew that he was in love with me. He did not push himself on me, because I could leave. See, I was so much younger. And I could leave. I am Polish, and this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he did not push himself. But I know that's the reason he allowed me to do this, and he said we will wait, and you know. So now, the time is coming that I am-- he said, I heard that the end is coming, you know. On every street corner in Poland, there was sign, this sign, this town is Jew-free. And whoever will help escaped Jew, the sentence is death. Only in Poland the signs were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, now I am-- know that the end is coming. One day I am in town. And like from nowhere, Gestapo is everyplace. Whoever was on the street, Polish, Ukrainian, they push us to one place. They force us to watch-- a Polish couple with two little babies, a Jewish couple with two children, they were hanging in the middle of the marketplace-- as a warning. We were forced to watch that. There is no way I can tell you how to-- the little children screaming. Then the fighting for breath. Even with closed eyes you can see, you can feel it. When I came to the villa, I mean, I don't know like zombie. How I get ever the strength to come to villa. I opened the door. I leave it locked, because usually I locked the door and turned the key in the lock. So when the Major would come, he could not walk in. He did have to ring the bell.

speaker: interviewee
text: This time I pulled the key out. And standing in the kitchen, four Jewish girls came out from the cellar to help me when I was alone. I was white like the snow. They asked me, what happened? How could I tell them? How? I could not tell them. Did you know why they were hung?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because the Jewish couple-- the Polish couple was hiding the Jewish couple. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- they special pull everybody to watch. There are signs, when you do that, that's what will happen. I knew. When I come to the villa, you know, I could not tell them why. We did not have time to start conversation-- the door opened. The Major opened the door, walked in the kitchen, and did see me with the women. He knew them. To this day, I feel his chin was shaking. The eyes big with unbelief. He trusted me so much, you know. He didn't say one word. He just turned around and walked very fast to the library where the telephone was. I did have to run after him. I knew that he will call the head of the Gestapo. I ran. I kneeled down. I was kissing his hands, I was holding to him, I was praying, I was pleading. I say, you're not a murderer. You didn't kill anyone. The innocent people, nobody has right to kill or murder.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, how did you dare to do so in my house behind my back? I trusted you. I gave you such a protection. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, they're innocent people. Nobody has right to kill or murder. Forgive me. I would do it again. Forgive me-- I did not have home to take them to my home. I did see a little softening in his old eyes. He said, Irene, I don't want it to see you dead. But I have my orders. I will go now to my office. I have to have time to compose myself. When I come back, I give my decision. In meantime, don't you dare to do anything stupid. He left. I ran downstairs. Closed the door. Everybody was ready to run out. I could not let them go during the day. So many people start going out. So we made a decision-- they will go underneath the gazebo. And if in, three days, I will not come, I am dead or arrested. They--they are on their own. They have to go from the cellar, through the window, and to the forest where other-- where the other Jewish people were hiding. We said goodbye. And I try as best as I know to look for that evidence that many people leave in their servant's quarters-- trying to-- to-- you know. And everybody, you train, you're doing, but with the evidence really come. There were sandwich laying here, the cigarettes there-- you know, it was spreaded it out. So I was trying to grab it like, trying to clean it up. About, oh, two hours, or maybe more, the Major come. He was drunk. He passed me by. Well, I needed to know. I follow him. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wait for the decision of life or death. He said, Irene, I cannot see you dead. I want you to be mine. And willingly, I'll even help. But you have to be mine. There was nothing else I could do. It was not easy, but it was small price to pay for so many lives. Not only the lives in the villa, but the people in the forest, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I give myself to him, and I could not even tell my people. Because how would they feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, that-- that only women. He did not ask who else was there. He was happy with me. They were only innocent women.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now that he knew, it was a little easier, you know? And you know that the women-- you had Ida, and Franka, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Heshu's wife-- I don't-- I have in a book. I forgot. They were even to the point that he, when he-- it was now [NON-ENGLISH], because the Russians were now fighting the Germans. And the light were-- so I painted all the glass with the paint, you know, in the entry hall, and this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Major, a few times, he did not go down to the cellar, but he opened the door and called, Franka, Ida. They made cookies for him. They made soup for him, when he was alone. They called him dziadek, Grandpa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he, after that, knew that they are still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew that the women were there. He did not know about men. He didn't ask and I didn't tell him. Now, though, he was telling me more. And he told me that the Russian fighting viciously, and that they are coming closer to Tarnopol. And as soon as they come, I have to do something, you know, with the women. So that gives me a hint.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went to [NON-ENGLISH], another part of Ukraine, for three days. At that time, I connected with Helen. She brought the slate and the horse. Henry Weinbaum put Major's uniform-- his hat, his coat. Because now many of the more soldiers were coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: He spoke very good German, and he was tall like the Major, you know-- sort of physique the same. I was sitting next to the German Major. A Jewish man in a Major's uniform. We put three men on the bottom of the slate, covered them with the blankets. We put shovel and the other things for them to dig bigger bunker underneath the trees in forest for the women. I made it twice trip to the forests. Six men were there. The Major did not know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So passed now a garage, and we're coming closer. Really, you can hear shooting, explosion. Now everybody-- Polish, Ukrainian-- tried to leave the town, because the front was supposed to be now in Tarnopol.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Helen brought that slate and a horse one day. Some kind of fly-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can hear, I mean, I did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- Helen brought that state and a horse. We wrapped the women in the blankets, and we put the-- we put the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] chairs, valises, luggage. We looked like everybody else, running out of town. Everybody was running. So it was not as dangerous-- I was alone. I brought them to the forest, and I remained with them. I befriended, in the forest, Henry Pachovski. Polish, he is partisan, and also Polish forest ranger. I made it, before, friends with him, and brought him things, and this, and I was alone, and I loved to go pick up mushrooms and berries, and this and that. I didn't-- he didn't tell me that he was with partisan. I did not tell him what I was doing. You know, we were friends. But that gave me excuse, many times, to go there-- to the forest. And I even told the Major that I find a nephew. You know, and part of my family. And I did not expect it, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So many times, I asked if I can take this or that to the forest. He said that he even himself, you know, helped me to bring it, something to the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now the time is coming that I have to-- he said, that soon I will be leaving, because the front is so close. You have to get-- you have to take the women out. Because I don't want the Germans to-- to-- the German to find my-- in my home, Jews. So I promise him.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, they brought it-- he went someplace, and I took them to the forest. Now it's March 15, 1944. Or was it March the 10th, '44, first?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you could see-- I am with the people. I am staying in the Henry Pachovski home, as a cousin. But during the day, I was going into the little holes there, bringing and looking, and this. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we heard that, now, by the short radio from England, we heard that-- that the German pushed the Russian again from Tarnopol, pushed them out. I am sitting, in the morning-- oh, Ida and Franka-- you know, Ida was heavy with the child. She would need hole in the ground by herself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was here in forest to tell Pachovski that I am hiding two women and that one is pregnant. And I am scared to put her in the forest in a hole because she will die. So he did-- he was connected with England. He did have a special room that nobody know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He sent his wife, with two children, he sent them to grandmother. And that day, I brought Franka and Ida to his home. He put them. And the rest was in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the war, they are fighting back and forth, the Russians. I am sitting at the kitchen window. Who is coming? Major, with the [NON-ENGLISH], He was so worried that the Russians would kill me. He loved me, the Major.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not tell him about the Jewish people. But they-- now, he asked the help that I am at the cousin. I am sitting and they're coming. Henry Pachovski got white. Because he said, you know, the Gestapo. I said, one minute-- let me run out. I run out. I run out, I say, oh, I'm so glad you come. I was so scared of the Russians. You know, I told you that I will run out to my-- to my cousin and this. And in the meantime, the wife of Henry brought coffee, and the coffee, and brought it out to the car. They didn't-- they say they have to leave, because they are in transit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he made it a special the few miles to pick me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could not. I went only to Ida and to-- because she expecting baby any day. And I said, I have to leave. And-- and there was nothing else I can do, you know, for safety of all people. So now I am leaving. I am leaving-- in the hiding, in the car, of the Major in this [NON-ENGLISH]. Those-- that men, have it-- he did have a connection for partisans. And that was-- on the way, I was-- we were leaving, you know. And he gave me the name of the partisans.

words.text: else I can do, you know,

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the way, Major left. He did go, put me in a hotel and I disappeared. And that was the last time I did see the Major.
